message.content:
<deliberation>
I will evaluate each requested GDPR node against the provided policy chunk. For every main node, I identify its relevant cross-references from the provided referenced_nodes (and, where necessary, other main nodes if the referenced text is included there). Omissions in the company policy are treated as Non-Deviation per instruction 4. I only flag deviations where the policy text narrows, negates, or otherwise alters a GDPR requirement relative to the quoted legal text (instruction 4.1). I found three clear deviations:
- Art 15(1): the policy frames access around “data we store,” which is narrower than GDPR’s “data being processed” (Data deviation).
- Art 21(1): the policy allows objections only where processing is based on Art 6(1)(f), omitting Art 6(1)(e) (Data deviation).
- Art 17(1): the policy lists only two of the six GDPR grounds for erasure, narrowing the scope (Data deviation).

All other nodes are treated as Non-Deviation due to silence/omission or neutral wording that does not contradict or alter the GDPR constraints.

Now I will output one JSON object per main node with considered references and any deviations classified using the required taxonomy.
</deliberation>
```json
[
  {
    "reg_id": "REG-021",
    "clause": "Art15(1)",
    "considered_references": ["REG-041"],
    "deviation_found": true,
    "deviations": [
      {
        "deviation_type": "Data deviation",
        "analysis": "GDPR Art 15(1) grants confirmation about whether data \"are being processed\" and access to the personal data and specified information: \"The data subject shall have the right to obtain from the controller confirmation as to whether or not personal data ... are being processed, and, where that is the case, access to the personal data and the following information: (a)...(h) including profiling referred to in Article 22(1)...\" (REG-021; with Art 22(1) cross‑reference REG-041). The policy limits the scope to data \"we store\" about the data subject, which narrows GDPR’s broader concept of processing (which includes collection, use, transmission, etc.). This constitutes a data-scope narrowing relative to the regulatory phrase \"are being processed\"."
      }
    ]
  },
  {
    "reg_id": "REG-031",
    "clause": "Art20(1)",
    "considered_references": ["REG-001", "REG-009"],
    "deviation_found": false,
    "deviations": []
  },
  {
    "reg_id": "REG-035",
    "clause": "Art21(1)",
    "considered_references": ["REG-001"],
    "deviation_found": true,
    "deviations": [
      {
        "deviation_type": "Data deviation",
        "analysis": "GDPR Art 21(1) provides the right to object to processing based on Art 6(1)(e) or (f): \"...to processing of personal data ... which is based on point (e) or (f) of Article 6(1)...\" (REG-035; cross‑ref to lawful bases in REG-001). The policy only grants the right to object when processing is based on Art 6(1)(f), omitting processing based on Art 6(1)(e). This narrows the conditions under which objections may be raised compared to the GDPR."
      }
    ]
  },
  {
    "reg_id": "REG-025",
    "clause": "Art17(1)",
    "considered_references": ["REG-035", "REG-036", "REG-005", "REG-009"],
    "deviation_found": true,
    "deviations": [
      {
        "deviation_type": "Data deviation",
        "analysis": "GDPR Art 17(1) lists six independent grounds requiring erasure: \"...where one of the following grounds applies: (a) ... no longer necessary; (b) the data subject withdraws consent ... and where there is no other legal ground; (c) the data subject objects to the processing pursuant to Article 21(1) ... or ... Article 21(2); (d) ... unlawfully processed; (e) ... to be erased for compliance with a legal obligation; (f) ... collected in relation to the offer of information society services referred to in Article 8(1).\" (REG-025; cross‑refs to Art 21(1)/(2) in REG-035/REG-036; Art 8(1) in REG-005; and Art 9(2)(a) as cited within REG-009). The policy states erasure applies only if data are no longer needed or consent is withdrawn, omitting the other four GDPR grounds. This narrows the scope of the right to erasure compared to GDPR’s full set of grounds."
      }
    ]
  },
  {
    "reg_id": "REG-020",
    "clause": "Art14(5)",
    "considered_references": ["REG-098", "REG-016"],
    "deviation_found": false,
    "deviations": []
  },
  {
    "reg_id": "REG-010",
    "clause": "Art9(3)",
    "considered_references": ["REG-009"],
    "deviation_found": false,
    "deviations": []
  },
  {

    "clause": "Art13(1)",
    "considered_references": ["REG-001", "REG-078", "REG-079", "REG-080", "REG-081", "REG-082", "REG-083", "REG-084", "REG-085", "REG-086"],
    "deviation_found": false,
    "deviations": []
  },
  {
```
